title: QUIC RFC 9000 NEW_CONNECTION_ID Frame Codec Architecture
purpose: Describe the bounded NEW_CONNECTION_ID frame-codec floor that parses and formats the type, sequence number, Retire Prior To, connection ID length, connection ID, and stateless reset token fields while rejecting malformed codec-local layouts.
design_summary: The frame codec accepts NEW_CONNECTION_ID frame type 0x18 and decodes the following variable-length integer fields in order: Sequence Number, Retire Prior To, and Length. The codec then admits only connection ID lengths from 1 through 20 bytes, requires the remaining payload to contain the declared connection ID plus a 16-byte stateless reset token, and rejects Retire Prior To values greater than the Sequence Number. Formatting applies the same local shape constraints before emitting type 0x18, the two variable-length integer fields, one length byte, the connection ID, and the stateless reset token.
data_and_state_considerations: NEW_CONNECTION_ID codec state is frame-local. The codec returns borrowed spans over the input payload and does not own the peer connection-ID registry, active_connection_id_limit enforcement, migration routing, retire-prior-to orchestration, stateless-reset token routing, or connection-close datagram emission.
edge_cases_and_constraints: The Type field is constrained to NEW_CONNECTION_ID value 0x18 for this parser entry point.
Sequence Number and Retire Prior To remain QUIC variable-length integers.
Connection ID length values of zero and greater than 20 are rejected by the codec.
Truncated connection ID or stateless reset token payloads are rejected.
Retire Prior To greater than Sequence Number is rejected by both parse and format paths.
Deterministic fuzz coverage exercises representative valid frame shapes and truncation rejection.
alternatives_considered: Leave NEW_CONNECTION_ID covered only by the broad RFC 9000 transport artifact; rejected because the active proof needed direct requirement-home links and benchmark coverage for this wire-facing codec.

Combine the frame-codec proof with runtime peer connection-ID registry behavior; rejected because duplicate detection, active limit handling, retirement, and migration routing are separate state-machine slices.
risks: Treating this codec proof as full connection-ID lifecycle support would overstate behavior.
Accepting invalid length or truncated token payloads would allow malformed NEW_CONNECTION_ID frames through the parser.
Allowing Retire Prior To to exceed Sequence Number would weaken the RFC 9000 local frame-shape invariant.

supplemental_sections.heading: Scope
supplemental_sections.content: This design covers only NEW_CONNECTION_ID frame-codec parsing, formatting, deterministic fuzz coverage, and benchmark harness coverage for RFC 9000 Section 19.15 field layout and codec-local validation. It does not claim active connection-ID limit management, duplicate sequence or duplicate value policy, peer CID registry updates, migration routing, stateless-reset response behavior, RETIRE_CONNECTION_ID orchestration, connection-close datagram emission, public API controls, or interop-runner migration success.
